metrics.title: Liquidity
metrics.label: Same-day settlement; zero drawdown risk on principal

moat.description: Cash is held as a dynamic hedge and reinvestment reserve — its weight rises when valuations are stretched and shrinks when high-quality opportunities emerge. The USD itself benefits from universal settlement status and zero principal drawdown.
moat.analysisSummary: Cash's role is as a **Crash Hedge, Dry Powder, and Volatility Buffer** — not as a long-term holding:

analysisPoints.title: Universal Settlement
analysisPoints.text: The USD denominates ~88% of global FX transactions and ~58% of central bank reserves. Every other asset class is ultimately priced and settled in dollars, giving cash a structural network advantage that takes decades to erode.

analysisPoints.title: Zero Nominal Drawdown
analysisPoints.text: Unlike any other asset, money-market and short-duration Treasury holdings carry no mark-to-market volatility on principal. In a 2008- or 2020-style dislocation, cash buyers transact at the bottom while levered holders are forced sellers.

analysisPoints.title: Positive Real Yield
analysisPoints.text: With 1-month T-bills at ~4.3% and headline CPI at ~2.7%, cash delivers a positive real yield for the first sustained period in 15 years. The opportunity cost of holding cash is the lowest it has been since the 2007 cycle.

analysisPoints.title: Optionality Value
analysisPoints.text: Cash is the only asset whose value rises in expectation when other asset prices fall. A 20% S&P drawdown effectively raises the future return on every dollar held in reserve — a feature no productive asset can replicate.

growth.description: Cash does not compound through reinvestment — it earns the prevailing risk-free rate. With Fed funds at 4.25–4.50% and short Treasuries yielding ~4.3%, holders capture ~1.7% real return after CPI. The growth thesis is purely the persistence of the risk-free rate; if the Fed cuts aggressively into 2026–27, the real return compresses fast.

growthAnalysis.scoreDerivation: Base ~61 (4-5% nominal CAGR is mid-band) + 0 (drivers stable) + 0 (margin stable) + 0 (no TAM expansion — yield is mechanical) - 5 (moderate inflation/real-yield risk) = ~56

drivers.name: Money market AUM
drivers.metric: US MMF assets ~$7.0T (record high) — reflects sustained demand for risk-free yield

growthAnalysis.keyRisk: Fed pivots dovish in H2 2026 amid growth slowdown; cuts 100–150 bps over 12 months, dragging T-bill yields to ~3% and turning real yield negative if CPI stays sticky.

valuation.description: Cash trades at par by definition — there is no over- or under-valuation, only opportunity cost. With short yields at ~4.3% and equity earnings yields at ~3.8% (S&P 500 forward), the spread favours cash for the first time since 2007. Valuation 'score' anchored at the base case (par) — cash cannot be 'cheap' or 'expensive' in price terms, only in relative-yield terms.

bear.description: Fed cuts aggressively into a soft-landing or mild recession, dragging T-bill yields to ~3% while CPI remains sticky around 2.7%, compressing real yield toward zero.
points: Fed funds rate cut 125–150 bps over 12 months
Real yield approaches zero or turns slightly negative

base.description: Fed holds at current restrictive levels through most of 2026; short rates persist around 4.3–4.5% as inflation moderates gradually but stays above target.
points: Fed funds held at 4.25–4.50% for most of 2026
Money-market yields stay in the 4.30–4.60% range

bull.description: Reaccelerating inflation or tariff-driven price shocks force the Fed to hold or modestly hike; cash holders capture sustained 5%+ nominal yield with strong real returns.
points: CPI reaccelerates above 3% on tariffs / energy shock
Fed signals 'higher for longer' or one additional 25 bps hike
Real yield expands toward +2.5% as nominal yields rise faster than CPI

learnedInterfaces.note: N/A — cash has no interface or UX surface to learn.

businessLogic.note: N/A — cash is a unit of account, not a business with proprietary logic.

publicDataAccess.note: N/A — yields and rates are fully public; no proprietary data dynamic applies.

talentScarcity.note: N/A — holding cash requires no specialist talent.

bundling.note: N/A — cash is a singular fungible asset, not a bundleable product.

proprietaryData.note: N/A — there is no proprietary data layer to a fiat unit of account.

regulatoryLockIn.note: USD is US legal tender, the unit of US tax obligations, and the dominant settlement currency for global trade and commodities — embedded in regulatory frameworks worldwide.

networkEffects.note: USD remains the dominant global FX (~88% of transactions) and reserve currency (~58% of reserves), but reserve share has slowly drifted from ~65% in 2014 as BRICS+ pursue de-dollarisation.

transactionEmbedding.note: Cash is the transaction layer itself — every wage, rent, tax, and trade is denominated and settled in it. Switching costs to any alternative are effectively infinite at the system level.

systemOfRecord.note: The USD is the unit of account for nearly all global financial records, contracts, and price discovery. No other store of value comes close to its system-of-record status.

tenMoats.verdict: Cash's moat is structural and AI-immune — AI cannot displace the unit of account in which AI services themselves are priced. The slow erosion comes from de-dollarisation and inflation, not technological disruption.
